name: NEW YORK TIMES COQUITO TRES LECHES CAKE

ingredients.item: Nonstick spray or coconut oil, for pan

method: I call this my New York Times tres leches cake because this cake was featured in a video I did with the New York Times cooking channel on YouTube, which really helped propel my career. It’s still on YouTube, if you want to check it out. I was interviewed by Priya Krishna, who took the time to highlight my business and show the world how one person was making everything happen. This cake is so special to eat during the holidays—it contains coquito, which is a popular Puerto Rican eggnog drink. I’m so happy I get to share this exclusive recipe with you.
Preheat the oven to 375°F (190°C) and oil a 9 x 13–inch (23 x 33–cm) baking pan with nonstick spray or coconut oil.
In a large bowl, sift the flour, add the granulated sugar, baking powder and salt and mix.
Add the almond milk, oil and vanilla and whisk until combined. Do not overmix.
Pour the batter into the prepared baking pan and bake for 20 to 25 minutes. Insert a toothpick into the center to see whether it comes out clean—if not, let bake for a little longer.

In a blender, combine your coconut milk, condensed coconut milk, evaporated coconut milk, nutmeg and vanilla. Pour into a pot over low heat and add the cinnamon sticks, star anise, cloves and a pinch of salt, and let simmer, keeping an eye on it and stirring occasionally, for about 10 minutes, or until all the spices smell fragrant. Once it is done, remove from the heat and set aside to cool.

In a small pot over medium heat, combine the aquafaba, granulated sugar and agar agar. Heat until it reaches a low boil, then remove from the heat and transfer to a heatproof bowl. Let cool slightly, until you can put your finger into it. In a stand mixer, blend the aquafaba mixture on high speed for 7 minutes. You will see it start to turn white and build volume. Turn off the blender, then add the cream of tartar and powdered sugar and mix on high speed for another 7 minutes, while gradually adding the vanilla. At this point, you should have a thick and creamy whipped cream. Refrigerate the whipped cream until you are ready to use it. Alternatively, you can wait to make the whipped cream until the next day, when you frost the cake after letting it sit overnight.

When the cake has cooled, cut off all the edges and use a fork to poke many holes throughout the cake. Pour about a cup (240 ml) of your coquito leche all over the cake and let the cake absorb as much as possible, then add another cup (240 ml) and save the rest of the coquito for later.
Cover the cake and refrigerate for 2 hours to overnight.
The next day, add your whipped cream and garnish with coconut flakes. Cut and serve.
